question.text: An investor has a 15-year retirement goal. His financial adviser suggests "time in the market" rather than "timing the market." This advice implies:

answer.text: The investor should invest only when markets are at their lowest
The investor should switch between equity and debt based on market levels
Staying invested consistently over long periods is more important than trying to predict market highs and lows
The investor should redeem and re-enter the market frequently to capture short-term gains

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** "Time in the market" emphasizes staying invested consistently over long periods, allowing the power of compounding and market growth to work. "Timing the market" -- trying to predict when to enter and exit -- is extremely difficult even for professionals. Missing just a few of the best market days can dramatically reduce long-term returns. For a 15-year goal, consistent investing through SIPs regardless of market conditions is generally superior.
**The Trap:** Investing only at market lows IS market timing (and nearly impossible to execute consistently). Switching between asset classes based on market predictions is also timing. Frequent redemption and re-entry involves transaction costs and taxes, and the risk of being out of the market during rallies.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Studies consistently show that even professional fund managers struggle to time markets successfully over extended periods. The opportunity cost of being in cash when markets rally sharply (which often happens suddenly after crashes) outweighs the benefit of avoiding some downturns. This is why SIP investing is advocated for long-term goals.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Show clients the impact of missing the best market days. Missing just the 10 best days over a 20-year period can reduce cumulative returns by 40-50%. This powerfully illustrates why staying invested (time IN the market) matters more than trying to time entry and exit points.
